topics.brief: Why everyone needs a digital executor — someone authorized to manage your digital accounts, passwords, and files after death or incapacity. Practical steps to appoint one.
Why a password manager alone won't save your family after you die — and what you actually need.
Your Spotify playlists, Netflix profile, and subscriptions — what happens to them when you die, and why nobody thinks about this.
The 5 most common estate planning mistakes expats make — and how to avoid them.
2FA protects your accounts while you're alive — but locks everyone out when you die. How to plan for this.
Can you make a legally valid digital will? What's the difference between a digital will and storing your paper will digitally?
Your family photos are trapped in cloud accounts. Here's what happens to them when you die — and how to make sure they survive.
GDPR gives you the right to your data. But does that right transfer to your heirs? The legal grey zone that affects every European.

What happens to your Stripe balance or Shopify store when you die? For many entrepreneurs, their business is their biggest asset, yet its digital keys are often lost. How to ensure your online business survives you.
A complete guide to setting up legacy contacts and memorialization settings on major social platforms. Don't leave your profile to chance.

Digital nomads live in one country, work in another, and have servers in a third. When they pass away, which laws apply to their digital assets? A guide to the jurisdictional complexity of digital legacy for the modern remote worker.

A 2026 update on digital inheritance laws across Europe. What's changed, what's still a grey area, and how the new EU regulations impact your digital estate. Comparing Germany, France, Netherlands, and Spain.
Your WhatsApp chats vanish when you die. Unlike photos and emails, messaging apps offer zero options for legacy access. What this means for your digital footprint.
You unlocked your phone with your fingerprint — but when you die, your family can't. The biometric security problem nobody talks about in digital estate planning.
Your hardware wallet holds €100,000 in Bitcoin, but nobody knows the PIN. A brutal reality check for crypto estate planning in Europe.
German Erbrecht is centuries old — but it wasn't written for cloud accounts, cryptocurrency, or digital businesses. What German law actually says about your online legacy.
French succession law requires a notaire, but what about your Gmail? How French law treats digital assets, and why notaires are scrambling to adapt.
Italian inheritance law (successione) is family-focused, but digital assets weren't invented yet. Where your digital estate fits in Italy's legal framework.
Spanish notarios traditionally handle inheritance (herencia). Now they're facing digital assets nobody planned for. A guide to inheritance law in Spain for digital-first expats.
Google lets you plan for your Gmail with the Inactive Account Manager. Microsoft is slower. Yahoo never replying. A country-by-country guide to email access after death.
Your family shares your Apple One subscription — but when you die, what happens to the shared Netflix, iCloud storage, and app library?
You worked 15 years in Germany, 8 in the Netherlands, and now live in Spain. When you die, which country's pension laws apply? A guide to cross-border pension death benefits in the EU.
Your therapist's notes, your psychiatric evaluations, your meditation app history — all in the cloud. Privacy, inheritance, and who should have access when you die.
Your domain name (and everything on it) is your digital real estate. Unlike physical real estate, inheritance law barely touches it. How to ensure your domains pass to your heirs.
You run your own mail server, personal wiki, or side project on a VPS. When you die, who pays for the hosting? Who has the root password? A checklist for self-hosted digital assets.
You use your work Google account for personal files, photos, and documents. When you leave — or die — your employer can lock you out. How to separate work and life.
Your will only works after you die. But what if you're incapacitated — in a coma, dementia, or just unreachable? A power of attorney for digital assets handles this, but most people don't have one.
